content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural grade reorganization (lower grades stay, upper grades centralize)'. Rural primary school closures in Hungary would disproportionately harm low-income and Roma students through increased dropout risk and long-term earnings loss, unless accompanied by carefully designed consolidation (multi-grade organization, not exodus-inducing hub schools) and genuine transport/resource equity safeguards.
opportunities: Enables full-time specialist subject teaching at the centralized upper-grade site, addressing the documented Polish problem of rural teachers commuting between multiple part-time postings.

Preserves a local school presence for the youngest, most travel-vulnerable children (grades 1-4/1-6), partially mitigating the documented burden of early-morning long-distance travel for young pupils.
strengths: More than 1,200 of 3,155 Hungarian settlements (38%) had no public educational institutions by 2024, creating territorial exclusion from primary education access.

Disadvantaged Roma children are increasingly concentrated in rural settlements (up to 94% in some areas), while rural schools face disproportionate teacher shortages with 2–3 times higher rates of unqualified teachers than non-disadvantaged schools.
threats: Poland's 1999 reform was politically reversible and was in fact reversed after 17 years; without concrete cross-party entrenchment mechanisms or constitutional-level protection, Hungary's version faces similar reversal risk, especially given the documented absence of any cross-party consensus mechanism for rural school reform.

Teacher redeployment under Act LII of 2023 (Status Law), which grants district centers authority to reassign teachers, may trigger the kind of mobilization seen in the Tanítanék movement if perceived as involuntary displacement without adequate consultation.
weaknesses: The direct causal effect of rural school closures on social mobility in Hungary has not been empirically quantified; evidence is drawn from Chile and Serbia (non-Hungarian contexts) or correlational Hungarian data.

The degree to which small-class peer effects (which favor disadvantaged pupils) versus economies of scale (which favor consolidation) net out in Hungarian rural contexts is unknown; no cost-benefit or randomized trial exists for this trade-off in Hungary.
